title: Malware protection and update compliance for CE scope

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CE.SAU.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that Cyber Essentials CE.SAU.1 (Secure authentication & access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.27.30: Malware protection and update compliance for CE scope.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.27.30' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails (AWS, Azure), network infrastructure logs, financial application logs, identity management systems, vulnerability scan outputs, incident response logs, operational resilience evidence
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=defender sourcetype="DefenderATP:Alert" OR index=crowdstrike sourcetype="crowdstrike:hosts" earliest=-7d
| stats latest(last_seen) as ls, latest(rtr_state) as rtr by hostname
| eval stale=if((now()-ls)>604800,1,0)
| lookup cyberessentials_endpoints.csv hostname OUTPUT in_scope
| where in_scope="true" AND (stale=1 OR rtr!="enabled")
| table hostname, ls, rtr, stale
description: Supports UK Cyber Essentials supervisory expectations by correlating technical telemetry with governance records for malware protection and update compliance for ce scope.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for Cyber Essentials compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes crowdstrike, defender and sourcetypes DefenderATP:Alert, crowdstrike:hosts. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `uk_oes_inventory.csv` with OES identifiers, essential services, and CAF mappings. (2) Tag correlation searches with `nis_relevant` or `fca_relevant` for workflow routing. (3) Integrate IR ticketing milestones for competent authority and FCA clocks. (4) Monthly review with compliance, technology risk, and resilience forums. Indexes required: `index=crowdstrike`, `index=defender`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=DefenderATP:Alert`, `sourcetype=crowdstrike:hosts`.
visualization: Time chart (event volume), Heatmap (service x control), Table (top risk drivers). Key fields: `hostname`, `ls`, `rtr`, `stale`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.dest | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Operational resilience tests during planned exercises, scheduled reviews, or DR drills.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails (AWS, Azure), network infrastructure logs, financial application logs, identity management systems, vulnerability scan outputs, incident response logs, operational resilience evidence.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `uk_oes_inventory.csv` with OES identifiers, essential services, and CAF mappings. (2) Tag correlation searches with `nis_relevant` or `fca_relevant` for workflow routing. (3) Integrate IR ticketing milestones for competent authority and FCA clocks. (4) Monthly review with compliance, technology risk, and resilience forums.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=defender sourcetype="DefenderATP:Alert" OR index=crowdstrike sourcetype="crowdstrike:hosts" earliest=-7d
| stats latest(last_seen) as ls, latest(rtr_state) as rtr by hostname
| eval stale=if((now()-ls)>604800,1,0)
| lookup cyberessentials_endpoints.csv hostname OUTPUT in_scope
| where in_scope="true" AND (stale=1 OR rtr!="enabled")
| table hostname, ls, rtr, stale
```

Understanding this SPL

**Malware protection and update compliance for CE scope (Cyber Essentials)** — Supports UK Cyber Essentials supervisory expectations by correlating technical telemetry with governance records for malware protection and update compliance for ce scope.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails (AWS, Azure), network infrastructure logs, financial application logs, identity management systems, vulnerability scan outputs, incident response logs, operational resilience evidence. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: defender, crowdstrike; **sourcetype**: DefenderATP:Alert, crowdstrike:hosts. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=defender, index=crowdstrike, sourcetype="DefenderATP:Alert", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **hostname**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **stale** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where in_scope="true" AND (stale=1 OR rtr!="enabled")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Malware protection and update compliance for CE scope (Cyber Essentials)** — Supports UK Cyber Essentials supervisory expectations by correlating technical telemetry with governance records for malware protection and update compliance for ce scope.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, cloud audit trails (AWS, Azure), network infrastructure logs, financial application logs, identity management systems, vulnerability scan outputs, incident response logs, operational resilience evidence. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: hostname, ls, rtr, stale.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the GRC system and the audit register, and with regional reporting cutoffs in your runbook. Document any expected regulatory reporting window or approved exception before escalating.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (event volume), Heatmap (service x control), Table (top risk drivers).
grandmaExplanation: We help you show United Kingdom financial and operational resilience rules are met with clear evidence from your live systems.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
